text: The direct selective functionalization of methane into valuable chemicals is a longstanding challenge in the field of catalysis. The production of acetic acid currently relies on the well-known and highlyenergycostly methanol carbonylation route using expensive homogeneous transition metal catalysts and is a highly environmentally conflicting process. Here, we develop and demonstrate a photocatalytic strategy for the selective conversion of CH 4 into acetic acid at ambient temperature using CO and water as feedstocks. At room temperature and under ambient pressure, using TiO 2 as a catalyst support and modifying it with ammonium phosphotungstic polyoxometalate (NPW) clusters and isolated single platinum atoms, we achieve the stable production of up to 5.7 mmol·L −1 acetic acid solution over 60 hours (based on liquid phase selectivity >90% and based on carbon atoms, 66% and an overall platinum catalyst turnover of 99 acetic acid moles catalyst −1 ). The influence of individual components of the catalyst and of water on the latter crucial for catalyst stability on the methane conversion and overall product selectivity is elucidated by using 13 CH 4 and in situ spectroscopy, thus showing that the overall process of the photocatalytic oxidative carbonylation of methane at Pt sites for acetic acid production is greatly enhanced by the catalyst-active sites synergy with water-derived hydroxyl radical species, which strongly enhance the methane activation process at the Pt 1 sites.
